title: Patchwork Dresser on Wheels
summary: A rolling five-drawer dresser with mismatched-width drawer faces — a plywood carcass, Euro slides throughout, and a single inset door for the odd-shaped top-corner opening.
description:
The "patchwork" look comes entirely from the drawer face widths — a mix of 1x4 and 1x8 boards rather than five identical faces — which means this isn't a simpler build than a matched-face dresser, just a different-looking one. Every joint underneath is the same precise plywood carcass work any Euro-slide dresser needs.

Euro slides are unforgiving of an out-of-square drawer box, and this design compounds that by inseting every drawer box 3/4" behind the case front to leave room for the applied drawer faces — so there are two things that both have to be right: the box itself square, and the inset consistent across all five drawers, or the faces won't sit in a flush, even plane once attached. Build and test-fit the drawer boxes in their openings *before* committing to faces, and don't skip the diagonal-measurement square check on the main case — everything downstream depends on it.

materials.name: Plywood, 1/4", 4x8 sheet
materials.note: Half sheet — drawer bottoms and backs.

cutList.note: Cut to fit slides and measured openings.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: From the 3/4" plywood sheet, rip 15-3/4"-wide strips and crosscut three case partitions at 24-3/4", one case top at 29-3/4", one small divider at 8-1/4", two sides at 48" and one cabinet door at 24-1/2". From the 1/4" plywood, cut the cabinet back at 48" x 26-1/4", two small drawer bottoms at 14" x 20-1/2", two wide drawer bottoms at 14" x 28-3/4" and one top drawer bottom at 14" x 6-1/2". Take the four wide drawer box fronts/backs at 27-1/4" from the 10 ft 1x6 and the six 14" drawer box sides plus the two 5" small sides from the 8 ft 1x6 — that second board is down to its last quarter inch, so cut it carefully and in that order. Cut the two narrow drawer faces at 21-1/4" from the 1x4, and the top face at 8" plus the two wide faces at 29-1/2" from the 1x8. The 1x3 will not cover everything asked of it: the four trim boards are 1-1/2" wide and total 102-1/2", but a 2-1/2"-wide 1x3 rips to only one usable 1-1/2" strip, so a single 8 ft board yields 96" — buy a second 1x3 before you also cut the narrow drawer fronts/backs at 14" and 19" from it.

steps.title: Read the case widths before you assemble anything
steps.body: Stop and reconcile the cut list before a single screw goes in, because it gives this carcass two different widths. The 1/4" cabinet back is 48" x 26-1/4", which only closes if the two 48" sides run the full height and the 24-3/4" case partitions sit between them — a case 26-1/4" wide outside with a 24-3/4" clear opening. But the case top and the long trim boards are 29-3/4", the wide drawer boxes come out 28-3/4" wide (their own 14" x 28-3/4" bottoms prove it) and the wide drawer faces are 29-1/2", all of which describe a case roughly three and a half inches wider. Nothing in the plan says which width is right, so decide it yourself before cutting anything else: measure the openings you actually build and size the drawer boxes and faces from those measurements, not from the cut list's wide-drawer numbers.

steps.title: Build the partition assembly
steps.body: Drill 3/4" pocket holes in both ends of each 24-3/4" case partition and in the top and bottom edges of the 8-1/4" small divider, then join the partitions to each other in the stack order your drawer layout calls for using 1-1/4" pocket hole screws and glue. Fit the small divider between two of the partitions to create the odd top-corner opening the inset cabinet door will close. Every joint gets a square check as it is driven — Euro slides magnify an out-of-square partition into a drawer that rubs on one side and gaps on the other, and there is no fixing it after the sides go on.

steps.title: Join the case top, bottom, and sides
steps.body: Stand the partition assembly between the two 48" side panels and pull it together with 3/4" pocket holes, 1-1/4" pocket hole screws and glue, then add the case top across the top. Keep the front edges of every partition dead flush with the front edges of the sides — the whole drawer-face plane is referenced off that front surface. Measure both diagonals across the front of the case and adjust until they match before the glue grabs; this is the square check the rest of the build depends on. Note that the case top is 29-3/4" long while the partitions are 24-3/4", so it will not sit flush between 3/4" sides — see the width contradiction flagged earlier and trim it to the case you actually built.

steps.title: Add the case trim
steps.body: Face the raw plywood edges with the four 1-1/2"-wide pine trim boards — the two 21-1/2" pieces and the two 29-3/4" pieces — attached with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket hole screws from behind so no fastener shows on the front. Cut them to the case you actually built rather than to the list, since the 29-3/4" figure belongs to the wider of the plan's two conflicting carcass widths. Hold each piece flush with the case front and clamp it before driving, so it cannot creep proud and stand in the way of the drawer faces later.

steps.title: Build the drawer boxes and install slides
steps.body: Measure each of the five openings you have actually built — height and width, front and back — and size each drawer box from those numbers and from the clearance your particular Euro slides specify, because the plan's own drawer parts do not agree with its carcass width. The parts as cut give a wide box 28-3/4" across (27-1/4" fronts and backs captured between 14" sides, confirmed by the 14" x 28-3/4" bottoms), a narrow-tall box 20-1/2" across, and a small top box 6-1/2" across. Build each box square with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" screws, glue the 1/4" plywood bottom on, and check the diagonals of every box before the glue sets — a Euro slide will not forgive a box that is out of square. Mount each slide pair so the box front finishes 3/4" back from the case front; that recess is the whole reason the applied faces can end up in one flush plane.

steps.title: Attach the drawer faces and door
steps.body: With the boxes running, hold each face against its box and shim it to an even reveal on all four sides before fixing it — two strips of scrap of the same thickness under and beside the face are the easiest spacers. Fasten from inside the drawer box, through the box front into the back of the face, so nothing shows from outside. Check the wide faces against your case first: at 29-1/2" they are wider than the 26-1/4" carcass the cabinet back describes, so on that reading they cannot be hung at all and must be cut to the openings you built. Hang the 24-1/2" plywood door on the full-inset concealed hinges last, and adjust the hinge cams until its gaps match the drawer reveals.

steps.title: Attach the back
steps.body: Pull the drawers back out, set the 1/4" plywood back onto the case, and check the case's diagonals one last time — the back is what locks the carcass square, so hold it to a square case rather than nailing it to a racked one. Glue the edges and every partition it crosses, then brad-nail it off with 1-1/4" brads, working from one corner outward so the panel cannot shift. Doing this after the drawers and door are fitted is deliberate: until the back is on you can reach inside to adjust a slide or a hinge.

steps.title: Add the casters
steps.body: Turn the case on its back and fasten a caster at each bottom corner, set in far enough from the edges that its mounting plate lands on solid plywood rather than on an edge. Stand it up and roll it on a flat floor before finishing — a dresser this tall on wheels wants all four casters bearing, and shimming one is far easier now than after the finish is on. Sand every surface through the grits, iron on edge banding wherever a plywood edge stays visible, and finish.

images.alt: Patchwork dresser on casters with mismatched drawer face widths
